"I've known Marc and Ben for a long time, and we got oh-so-close to partnering up at Uber in 2011. I blame Marc, Ben blames himself but let's just say it was on all of us and in 2017 Uber suffered the consequences of not having Marc on the board - iykyk."

7 minutes of Travis Kalanick and Ben Horowitz on how the story went down from both sides:

"I have this whole fundraising process that I would do just to perfection, and that means I'd run an auction."

"And I would do this thing where I would say, 'We don't know, this is gonna be one of the hottest deals in Silicon Valley this year. We don't know where the price is gonna be, where it's gonna go to, but it's at least blank.'"

"And I call this the uncapped anchor, meaning I'm never in a place where I'm negotiating with somebody where I'm here and they're here and we meet in the middle. I'm always saying, 'It's at least this, and it can go up.'"

"We got to 375 million pre with a16z."

Ben: "I was like, 'F***, I know we should do that deal. We lost the deal.'"

tweets.text:
BREAKING: US job postings on Indeed fell -3.5% YoY in the week ending July 10th, to near the lowest this year.

Postings have declined -37.2% since April 2022 and are now down to February 2021 levels.

The number of available vacancies in the US is now only +1.0% above pre-pandemic levels recorded in February 2020.

Furthermore, new job postings on Indeed dropped -10.9% YoY, to the lowest since early January, continuing their over 4-year decline.

As a result, new job postings are now -3.5% below February 2020 levels.

This all suggests a further deterioration in official BLS job openings data for June and July.

The US labor market is losing momentum.

thread_tweets.text:
A lot of newfags aren't aware of BitMEX, or the sheer magnitude of the exchange pre 2020. There was a period of time where BitMEX had over 80% market share for perpetual swaps, and nearly 90% of the open interest.

A lot of the crypto market structure we have today is due to a result of realtime testing on BitMEX.

Important to celebrate the fact BitMEX has never had a major security indecent, and largely has been a net positive for Bitcoin adoption over the years!

Impossible to argue they didn't propel this industry forward!

tweets.text:
gm

GME pumped last night on Robinhood Chain because it was paired with GME (the RWA).

Cool idea - buying the meme forces people to buy the RWA token, which theoretically increases demand in both and everyone wins.

Only issue is RWA have a real lack of liquidity on Robinhood atm - so when there are heavy demand events… there isn’t always enough supply that exists on chain to move the stock token in parity with what its value is on the market. This creates an arb opportunity for anyone who can mint the RWA on chain.

The “demand” created by the meme at these market caps and volume is a blip compared to  what the RWA stock actually trades.

So - we’re at the stage in Robinhood where any popular RWA asset pairs are very easy up, but should also reflexively be very easy down.

Honestly wouldn’t be surprised to see a few more RWA pairs squeeze today before things calm down (almost like aftershocks of an earthquake).

But - even though the “squeeze both” idea might not be incredibly valid today… there’s a real possibility it can be in the future when there is more volume and liquidity.

And in the meantime, it’s one hell of a marketing event to get people to bridge to Robinhood Chain.

So even though a lot of these pairs will correct sharply when the initial hype dies - the narrative and the momentum on chain is real and just getting started.

Still early.

tweets.text:
Personally, I am aggressively buying while most are maximum bearish right now because tokens are trading at all time lows and every chart looks dead.

Meanwhile we’re on calls with those exact same teams every week, and the picture behind the scenes couldn’t be more different. Many of them are quietly doing multi-million dollar revenues right now. Most of the market has no idea, simply because these things take years to build and none of it shows up on a chart until it does.

What almost nobody is pricing in: their pipelines are filling up.

Partnerships, integrations, product launches, enterprise deals. Founders are deliberately sitting on announcements right now because they know news gets absorbed and forgotten in a market like this. So they’re stacking it and waiting.

The moment sentiment turns even slightly, all of that comes out at once. Every team firing their news cascade into a market that’s finally paying attention again. That’s how you get reflexivity that catches everyone off guard.

This is exactly why we’re aggressively buying the most unobvious opportunities we can find while everyone else is midcurving, complaining, and giving up.

The setup is right in front of you. Most are just too exhausted to look at it.

tweets.text:
I have a tremendous amount of fond memories of BitMEX. 

It was where I first made my money. My first liquidation — my first margin call. 

I remember trading on BitMEX during the March Covid crash, frantically setting limit orders, panicking when the exchange finally froze. 

I remember looking at the orderbook seeing about zero dollars left on the bid side, and immediately trying to send in more money, because if the industry was going down, I was going down with it.

Thank you for all the memories. May you rest in peace.

tweets.full_text:
I have a tremendous amount of fond memories of BitMEX. 

It was where I first made my money. My first liquidation — my first margin call. 

I remember trading on BitMEX during the March Covid crash, frantically setting limit orders, panicking when the exchange finally froze. 

I remember looking at the orderbook seeing about zero dollars left on the bid side, and immediately trying to send in more money, because if the industry was going down, I was going down with it.

Thank you for all the memories. May you rest in peace.

tweets.text:
The biggest marketplace on Earth won't be the assets humans own. It'll be the data AI needs.

Everything that can be digitised is getting pulled in to train the next models. Every scientific dataset, every piece of research sitting in a university, every sensor reading off every John Deere tractor in a field. All of it feeds the machine, and almost none of it is visible to us.

What comes out is an agentic economy running at machine speed. You plug your data into a vault, and agents monetise it on your behalf, trading with other agents in real time, in a marketplace we never see.

The company sitting on proprietary data becomes part of it without even knowing.

This is what the Economic Singularity actually looks like from the inside. The real economy stops being the one we can see and price, and moves somewhere we can't follow.

You own a piece of it, or you get priced out of it.

@jvisserlabs.

tweets.text:
dispatch from Crucible Compute 

an under discussed reason for the H100 smile curve (from @ComputeDesk) 

deploying workloads on new hardware and new firmware is HARD and there is a shortage of low level kernel and compiler engineers to figure it out, nor can mid stage startups afford large compute orchestration teams to manage heterogenous infrastructure since it’s intermittent and expensive work

more efficient to run on H100s where you’ve already figured out how to make things work v burn expensive GPU time trying to refactor your workloads and solve firmware issues 

as hardware heterogeneity continues to increase across chips, networking, and more, huge opportunity to build abstraction layer(s) for how workloads get packaged and run on all types of hardware 

today there are lots of companies addressing one or a few abstractions (@SpectralCom for CUDA compilers, for example) but we expect this market to grow exponentially by necessity

if you’re building abstraction layers for both workload runtime, token pricing, workload timing arbitrage, or more, we’d love to meet you and learn more, potentially integrate you into our compute deployment as we learn by building our own little margin optimized token factory

if you’re building abstraction layers for both workload runtime, token pricing, workload timing arbitrage, or more, we’d love to meet you and learn more, potentially integrate you into our compute deployment as we learn by building our own little margin optimized token factory

tweets.text:
A willingness to try new things is the most important trait for any venture capitalist.

Too many try to eliminate all risk before they invest.

I do the opposite.

There are always big risks.

But I try to allow myself to experiment and invest in areas where other people fail to tread.

There had not been a successful Automobile company for 50 years when I backed Tesla.

Rockets were only thought of as government projects before SpaceX.

Web-based email didn't exist when we backed Hotmail. We just licked poison envelopes and prayed that our hand-written letters would arrive within a week or two.

One of our new portfolio companies is @colossal the company that's bringing back the woolly mammoth, the saber tooth tiger, and the dire wolf.

They're reinventing what a zoo looks like.

When you’re willing to fail small, you’re more likely to win big.

tweets.text:
Just learnt that Hermes might just be amazing at Oil Trading after all
​
4 Wins with ~63% ROI (albeit with a small bankroll)
​
Ran an experiment that lets Hermes auto-trade on Oil Daily Up Down market
​
Hermes started with $200 bankroll, leverages Synthdata daily forecast oil signals, and executes & settle via BlockRun x402
​
If Synth agrees with the market (positive delta) by ≥ 2 percentage point / disagrees with the market (negative delta) by ≤ 8 percentage point, Hermes executes the trade
​
Might just be luck during US-Iran escalation period but excited to see how Hermes performs over the next few months
​
(BlockRun comes in handy because it helps get over Polymarket Geoblock that users might face with CLOB API)
